responses.strategic_rationale: The project must reconcile high‑resolution parasomnia capture with limited staffing, rigorous ethics, and a constrained budget. Leveraging a hybrid monitoring cadence maximizes event yield while controlling costs. Introducing a semi‑automated detection pipeline reduces manual burden but requires careful calibration. Staffing restructuring ensures safety without exceeding labor budgets. A secure data‑sharing protocol expands scientific impact while respecting privacy. Tiered consent safeguards participant autonomy yet may limit data depth. Phased funding allocation protects against cost overruns but risks procurement delays. Together, these levers address the core tensions of data quality, operational feasibility, ethical compliance, and financial sustainability.

levers.name: Monitoring Cadence
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will shift the nightly monitoring schedule from uniform low‑burden recordings to a hybrid model that interleaves full PSG nights on selected participants. This increases data resolution for rare events but raises staffing demands during PSG nights and may extend participant stay duration. The trade‑off is higher event capture probability versus increased operational complexity and cost.
options: Deploy a hybrid monitoring schedule that balances low‑burden nightly recordings with targeted full PSG nights.
Implement a continuous real‑time anomaly detection pipeline that flags potential parasomnia events for immediate technician review.
Adopt a dynamic scheduling algorithm that reallocates PSG nights based on real‑time event density.
levers.review_lever: Hybrid monitoring balances data richness against staffing load; the options overlook how to manage participant fatigue from extended PSG nights.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to schedule monitoring nights

levers.name: Event‑Detection Architecture
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will replace manual event flagging with a computational detection system that processes multimodal streams in real time. This reduces technician workload and speeds up annotation, but introduces a risk of false positives that could inflate annotation volume and require additional human review. The trade‑off is faster throughput versus potential quality degradation if detection thresholds are not calibrated.
options: Build a rule‑based threshold system that triggers technician alerts when sensor metrics exceed predefined limits.
Train a supervised machine‑learning classifier on annotated PSG data to predict parasomnia episodes from multimodal streams.
Combine unsupervised clustering of sensor patterns with expert‑reviewed anchors to identify novel event signatures.
levers.review_lever: Automated detection promises efficiency but may miss subtle parasomnia signatures; the options do not address calibration against diverse patient phenotypes.
levers.lever_classification: methodology — which event‑detection algorithm to use

levers.name: Staffing Model
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will restructure night‑shift coverage to ensure continuous monitoring across all suites. This improves safety and responsiveness to events but may increase labor costs and require additional training for technicians. The trade‑off is enhanced participant safety versus higher personnel expenses.
options: Expand technician roster to include a dedicated night‑shift specialist who monitors all suites simultaneously.
Rotate a core team of technicians across nights to maintain continuity while sharing expertise.
Introduce a remote monitoring liaison who triages alerts and coordinates on‑site responses.
levers.review_lever: Expanding night coverage improves safety but increases labor costs; the options ignore potential burnout among technicians due to higher shift density.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to structure night‑shift coverage

levers.name: Data‑Sharing Protocol
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will establish a secure data‑sharing framework that allows external researchers to access de‑identified physiological data. This accelerates scientific collaboration and publication potential but imposes stricter compliance requirements and may delay data release. The trade‑off is broader dissemination versus increased administrative overhead.
options: Release de‑identified physiological datasets through a secure, access‑controlled repository with tiered permissions.
Provide anonymized summary statistics and event logs to collaborators under signed data‑use agreements.
Offer a controlled‑access data portal that requires institutional review board approval before download.
levers.review_lever: Secure data sharing fosters collaboration but adds compliance steps; the options assume all collaborators will meet institutional review board requirements without accounting for varying institutional capacities.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how to share data with external researchers

levers.name: Ethics & Consent Flow
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will implement a robust consent process that empowers participants to control data sharing levels. This enhances ethical compliance and participant trust but may reduce the volume of usable data for secondary analyses. The trade‑off is higher participant autonomy versus potential data scarcity.
options: Implement an electronic consent platform that records time‑stamped signatures and allows participants to withdraw at any time.
Conduct in‑person consent workshops that walk participants through data‑use scenarios and privacy safeguards.
Provide a tiered consent model where participants choose the level of data sharing they are comfortable with.
levers.review_lever: Tiered consent enhances autonomy but limits data depth; the options fail to consider how reduced data granularity might affect event‑triage algorithm training.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to obtain and manage participant consent

levers.name: Funding Allocation Strategy
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will allocate budget in a phased manner tied to milestone achievements, creating financial safeguards against overruns. This ensures fiscal discipline and protects the project from early cost escalations but may delay procurement of critical equipment if funding is withheld. The trade‑off is risk mitigation versus potential operational delays.
options: Allocate a fixed percentage of the annual budget to contingency reserves to absorb unforeseen renovation costs.
Prioritize early‑stage equipment procurement to lock in favorable pricing before inflation.
Schedule incremental funding releases tied to milestone achievements to maintain financial discipline.
levers.review_lever: Milestone‑based funding protects against overruns but may delay critical purchases; the options do not account for inflationary pressure on equipment prices during the renovation phase.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to allocate budget across phases

responses.strategic_rationale: These levers target the core trade‑offs of launching a residential parasomnia research unit: rapid, high‑quality recruitment; efficient suite utilization; scalable annotation; robust safety; controlled expansion; and continuous regulatory compliance. By addressing recruitment pathways, scheduling logic, semi‑automated scoring, real‑time risk monitoring, modular infrastructure, and audit readiness, the plan balances speed, cost, and scientific integrity. Each lever forces a decision that directly influences participant throughput, data fidelity, and operational risk, ensuring that the unit can meet its enrollment, event‑capture, and publication milestones while remaining compliant and financially viable.

levers.name: Participant Engagement Pathway
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever reshapes how participants are attracted and retained, directly affecting enrollment speed and data quality. A more engaging recruitment strategy can accelerate cohort build‑up but may introduce selection bias if certain demographics are over‑represented. Conversely, a less intensive approach preserves sample representativeness but risks falling short of enrollment targets, delaying downstream analyses.
options: Launch a community outreach program using local sleep clinics and social media, offering educational workshops and incentives for referrals.
Partner with patient advocacy groups to create a peer‑mentoring system where enrolled participants guide new recruits, fostering trust and adherence.
Deploy an interactive web portal that logs sleep diaries, provides personalized feedback, and rewards consistent participation with vouchers.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: speed of recruitment versus data quality; options overlook potential selection bias introduced by peer‑mentoring or digital engagement.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to recruit and retain participants

levers.name: Suite Utilization Optimization
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever determines how the eight active suites are scheduled, influencing throughput, staffing load, and participant experience. Optimized scheduling can maximize data capture per night but may compress technician coverage windows, increasing fatigue. Less aggressive utilization preserves staff flexibility but may leave suites idle, raising operational costs.
options: Adopt a staggered admission schedule that aligns participant stays with technician shift patterns, minimizing overnight coverage gaps.
Introduce a dynamic booking algorithm that reallocates suites in real time based on event frequency predictions, ensuring high‑yield participants occupy active suites.
Offer flexible stay extensions for participants with low event capture, reallocating suites to new arrivals during low‑activity periods to maintain utilization.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: maximizing suite occupancy versus participant comfort; options ignore possible noise from overlapping stays.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to schedule and allocate sleep suites

levers.name: Annotation Workflow Automation
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever changes the balance between manual labor and data readiness. Semi‑automated pre‑tagging speeds up annotation but may introduce systematic errors if the algorithm misclassifies events. A fully manual approach guarantees human oversight but delays data availability and increases costs.
options: Deploy a semi‑automated labeling pipeline that pre‑tags suspected events for human review, reducing annotation time by 40%.
Introduce a consensus‑driven double‑scoring system where two independent raters cross‑check each other’s annotations before final approval, enhancing reliability.
Implement a continuous learning feedback loop where scorer corrections are fed back into the algorithm to improve future pre‑tag accuracy.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: speed of annotation versus scorer reliability; options assume equal scorer expertise and ignore fatigue.
levers.lever_classification: methodology — how to process and score data

levers.name: Risk Mitigation Protocol
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever dictates the level of safety vigilance, affecting participant trust and operational flexibility. A high‑alert dashboard can prevent adverse events but may trigger unnecessary interventions, increasing staff workload. A lower‑alert threshold reduces staff burden but risks delayed responses to genuine emergencies.
options: Enforce a real‑time monitoring dashboard that alerts staff to abnormal physiological signals, enabling immediate intervention.
Establish a rapid response team that conducts on‑site safety drills monthly, ensuring readiness for emergencies.
Integrate a predictive analytics model that flags high‑risk participants before admission, allowing pre‑emptive safety measures.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: safety vigilance versus operational flexibility; options fail to account for false positives causing unnecessary interventions.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to manage safety and compliance

levers.name: Infrastructure Scalability Plan
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever determines how quickly the unit can grow to meet enrollment targets. Modular expansion preserves the domestic feel but may incur higher per‑suite costs. Repurposing adjacent spaces offers rapid capacity but could compromise privacy. Leasing a satellite site expands reach but introduces regulatory complexity.
options: Design modular suite units that can be added incrementally, allowing expansion to 12 suites without major renovation.
Repurpose adjacent residential spaces into temporary monitoring pods during peak recruitment periods, maintaining a domestic feel.
Lease a nearby commercial building for a satellite monitoring site, enabling parallel data collection while preserving the primary unit’s integrity.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: rapid expansion versus maintaining methodological rigor; options do not consider regulatory hurdles for satellite sites.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to expand capacity

levers.name: Regulatory Compliance Cadence
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever shapes the rhythm of ethics and regulatory interactions, influencing project speed and participant trust. Frequent internal audits keep the team prepared but consume resources. A centralized dashboard streamlines documentation but requires upfront development. A dedicated officer accelerates approvals but adds salary cost.
options: Schedule quarterly internal audits that simulate external reviews, ensuring continuous compliance readiness.
Create a centralized compliance dashboard that tracks all documentation, deadlines, and approvals in real time.
Engage a dedicated compliance officer to liaise with ethics boards, streamlining communication and reducing approval delays.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: thorough compliance versus project speed; options assume ethics boards will respond within standard timelines, ignoring potential delays.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how to maintain approvals

responses.strategic_rationale: The residential parasomnia unit hinges on balancing participant throughput, data fidelity, and operational sustainability. Core tensions arise between maximizing event capture and maintaining a low‑stress environment, between staffing efficiency and safety, and between rigorous ethics oversight and agile recruitment. The selected levers target these axes: retention strategies to reduce drop‑outs, flexible staffing to ensure continuous coverage, streamlined annotation to keep annotation backlog manageable, ethical cadence to preempt compliance delays, calibration protocols to preserve sensor accuracy, and funnel optimization to sustain enrollment targets. Together, they form a coordinated framework that directly influences the unit’s ability to meet its 36‑month success criteria while safeguarding participant welfare and data integrity.

levers.name: Participant Retention Strategy
levers.consequences: Implementing a structured retention plan reduces nightly dropout rates, thereby increasing the likelihood of capturing at least one adjudicated event per participant. Enhanced engagement also lowers the proportion of unproductive admissions, keeping the 20% ceiling in check. However, additional support activities may raise per‑participant costs and require extra staff time.
options: Introduce a nightly wellness check that offers personalized sleep hygiene tips and immediate feedback on sensor comfort, encouraging participants to remain in the unit for the full scheduled stay.
Offer a tiered incentive program that rewards participants for completing consecutive nights, with escalating monetary bonuses and complimentary health assessments at the end of the stay.
Deploy a peer‑support network where participants can share anonymized experiences via a secure messaging platform, fostering a sense of community and reducing perceived isolation.
levers.review_lever: The tension between participant comfort and data completeness is addressed, but none of the options explicitly account for the additional staffing burden of nightly wellness checks or the potential for incentive fatigue over longer stays.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to keep participants engaged throughout stay

levers.name: Night Shift Staffing Flexibility
levers.consequences: Adopting a flexible shift model allows technicians to respond promptly to escalation nights while maintaining adequate coverage of the 8 active suites. This reduces missed events and improves safety margins. The trade‑off is increased scheduling complexity and potential overtime costs if peak demand periods are misaligned with staff availability.
options: Implement a rotating 12‑hour shift schedule that aligns technician coverage with peak event‑capture windows, ensuring a technician is always on standby during the first, mid‑stay, and final nights.
Introduce a cross‑training program that enables a subset of technicians to perform both routine monitoring and full PSG setup, allowing rapid escalation without hiring additional staff.
Adopt a part‑time night‑shift pool that can be called in during high‑event periods, providing surge capacity while keeping base staffing costs lower.
levers.review_lever: The lever balances coverage and cost, yet the options overlook the impact of shift fatigue on annotation accuracy and the need for formal fatigue mitigation policies.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to schedule technicians for continuous coverage

levers.name: Data Annotation Workflow
levers.consequences: Streamlining annotation reduces the backlog of raw sensor data awaiting human scoring, thereby accelerating the release of usable datasets for Aim 2. Faster annotation also shortens the time to publish early results, supporting grant renewal. However, increased automation may introduce subtle biases if not properly validated against human scorers.
options: Deploy a tiered annotation pipeline where technicians first flag potential events using a lightweight rule‑based filter, then senior scorers review only flagged segments, cutting review time by up to 50%.
Introduce a peer‑review system where two technicians independently annotate the same segment and a third resolves discrepancies, ensuring consistency while distributing workload.

Implement a batch‑processing schedule that consolidates nightly data uploads into a single daily annotation session, allowing technicians to focus on high‑priority nights and reducing context switching.
levers.review_lever: The workflow improves efficiency, yet none of the options address how to monitor and correct for systematic annotation drift over the 3‑year period.
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to process annotations efficiently

levers.name: Ethical Oversight Cadence
levers.consequences: Setting a defined ethics review cadence ensures timely approvals for protocol amendments and participant safety updates, preventing operational delays. Regular oversight also reinforces participant trust and institutional compliance. The downside is that frequent reviews may slow responsiveness to emergent safety signals.
options: Schedule a formal ethics review every six months, with a standing sub‑committee empowered to approve urgent safety changes within 48 hours.
Establish a continuous monitoring dashboard that flags potential ethical concerns in real time, triggering an expedited review process when thresholds are crossed.
Create a joint ethics‑operations task force that meets monthly to pre‑emptively discuss upcoming protocol adjustments and align on risk mitigation strategies.
levers.review_lever: The cadence addresses approval timing, but the options do not consider the resource burden of maintaining a continuous monitoring dashboard or the potential for review fatigue among ethics board members.
levers.lever_classification: governance — how often ethics oversight occurs

levers.name: Equipment Calibration Protocol
levers.consequences: Regular calibration of dry‑electrode headbands, mattress sensors, and cameras preserves signal quality, directly influencing event detection accuracy. Consistent calibration also reduces false alarm rates, supporting the 20% unproductive admission threshold. The trade‑off is the need for scheduled downtime and additional technical staff time.
options: Implement a weekly calibration routine where each sensor type undergoes a standardized test against a reference signal, with results logged in a central quality‑control database.
Introduce a self‑diagnostic feature in the sensor firmware that alerts technicians to drift, allowing on‑the‑spot recalibration without full shutdown.
Schedule quarterly calibration workshops that bring in external experts to validate sensor performance and update calibration protocols based on emerging best practices.
levers.review_lever: Calibration improves data quality, yet the options fail to address how to handle calibration failures that occur during a participant’s stay without disrupting data collection.
levers.lever_classification: operations — how to maintain sensor accuracy

levers.name: Recruitment Funnel Optimization
levers.consequences: Optimizing the recruitment funnel increases the proportion of eligible participants who enroll, directly impacting the 50–70 participant target. A more efficient funnel also reduces the time and cost per enrollment. However, aggressive funnel tactics may raise concerns about participant selection bias.
options: Develop a targeted outreach campaign that partners with regional neurologists to provide pre‑screening webinars, filtering candidates before they reach the study portal.
Create a referral incentive program that rewards current participants for bringing in new eligible volunteers, leveraging peer networks to expand reach.

Implement an automated eligibility screener on the study website that guides potential participants through a structured questionnaire, flagging those who meet inclusion criteria for immediate scheduling.
levers.review_lever: The funnel strategies aim to boost enrollment, but none explicitly consider how to monitor and mitigate selection bias introduced by referral or self‑selection mechanisms.
levers.lever_classification: dissemination — how to attract participants

levers.name: Monitoring Cadence
levers.lever_classification: execution — how to schedule monitoring nights
levers.consequences: Pulling this lever will shift the nightly monitoring schedule from uniform low‑burden recordings to a hybrid model that interleaves full PSG nights on selected participants. This increases data resolution for rare events but raises staffing demands during PSG nights and may extend participant stay duration. The trade‑off is higher event capture probability versus increased operational complexity and cost.

Implement a continuous real‑time anomaly detection pipeline that flags potential parasomnia events for immediate technician review.

Implement an automated eligibility screener on the study website that guides potential participants through a structured questionnaire, flagging those who meet inclusion criteria for immediate scheduling.
levers.review: The funnel strategies aim to boost enrollment, but none explicitly consider how to monitor and mitigate selection bias introduced by referral or self‑selection mechanisms.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Classification**
   - `lever_classification`: a brief phrase with the category and what the lever decides.
   - Format: "category — what this lever decides".
   - Categories: methodology, execution, governance, dissemination, product, operations.
   - Examples: "governance — who oversees the review process", "methodology — which data collection approach to use", "execution — how to sequence the rollout phases".

3. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

4. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

5. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever`:
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Examples:
     - "Switching from seasonal contract labor to year-round employees stabilizes harvest quality, but none of the options price in the idle-wage burden during the 5-month off-season."
     - "Routing the light-rail extension through the historic district unlocks ridership but triggers Section 106 heritage review; the options assume permits will clear on the standard timeline."
     - "Pooling catastrophe risk across three coastal regions diversifies exposure on paper, but a regional hurricane season can correlate all three simultaneously — correlation risk absent from every option."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.

6. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

7. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach (at least 15 words with an action verb) — not a short label or vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Establish a 3-year residential longitudinal research unit in Bonn, Germany, for the study of adult NREM parasomnias — sleepwalking, confusional arousals, and sleep terrors — with a secondary exploratory arm for REM sleep behavior disorder under a separate protocol with dedicated neurologic screening, reflecting RBD's distinct clinical trajectory per current AASM guidance. The facility is affiliated with University Hospital Bonn's Department of Epileptology, leveraging its existing long-term video-EEG monitoring culture, and addresses a specific methodological gap: traditional single-night polysomnography rarely captures infrequent parasomnia events, while outpatient actigraphy lacks physiological resolution. The unit houses 8 active sleep suites at launch in a converted residential property in a quiet Bonn neighborhood, renovated for safety (padded corridor edges, restricted-opening windows, silent exterior door alarms, fire safety, acoustic treatment, network cabling, accessibility modifications) while preserving a domestic feel — if the environment is too clinical, naturalistic behavior is suppressed. Physical capacity exists for 12 suites, but expansion is deferred until the pilot proves acceptable data quality, manageable false alarm rates, safe staffing ratios, and usable annotation throughput. Sensing uses a tiered acquisition model: nightly low-burden monitoring via dry-electrode EEG headbands, contact-free mattress sensors, unobtrusively mounted infrared cameras, ambient microphones, and environmental sensors; scheduled enhanced nights with fuller PSG montage including standard scalp EEG, EOG, chin EMG, and leg EMG on the first night, one mid-stay night, and the final night per participant; and escalation nights deploying full PSG after technician-flagged events to capture recurrence at clinical-grade resolution. All streams are time-synchronized and stored in EEG-BIDS-compatible format on a local NAS with nightly encrypted backup to university storage, annotated in real time by the night technician and later scored by two independent raters using AASM criteria with inter-rater reliability reported. Public data sharing is limited to de-identified physiological and sensor data under data use agreements — bedroom video carries severe privacy constraints and is not deposited in public repositories. The scientific program has three explicit aims: Aim 1, establish and operationally validate the residential capture model as safe, ethics-approved, and sustainable; Aim 2, characterize within-person and between-person parasomnia patterns longitudinally, including episode frequency, morphology, triggers, and temporal clustering; Aim 3, develop and benchmark semi-automated event-triage tools that rank probable parasomnia episodes and reduce manual review burden, evaluated against dual human scorer agreement rather than promising autonomous diagnosis.

Participant recruitment targets adults aged 18–65 with NREM parasomnia confirmed through a structured pre-admission pathway: specialist clinical interview, collateral history from bed partner or household member where available, prior or screening polysomnography, and a structured exclusion checklist adjudicated by the PI. Explicit exclusions are nocturnal epilepsy or suspected epilepsy mimics, untreated obstructive sleep apnea, active major substance use disorder, severe psychiatric instability, and wandering or injury risk beyond facility capability. Recruitment channels are the University Hospital Bonn sleep clinic, regional neurologist referrals, and the Deutsche Gesellschaft für Schlafforschung und Schlafmedizin network, with per-night compensation of €80 plus meals. A matched control group provides baseline nocturnal movement, arousal patterns, and false-positive sensor activity under identical residential monitoring conditions, enabling benchmarking of the event-triage tools against normal nocturnal behavior. Event-yield planning assumes that based on the inclusion criterion of at least 2 self-reported episodes per month, approximately 70–80% of admitted participants will produce at least one captured and adjudicated event during a 2–8 week stay; stays may be extended up to 2 additional weeks for participants with zero captured events after the initial period, capped at 10 weeks total, with a protocol ceiling of 20% unproductive admissions before triggering a recruitment-criteria review. The core team is 9 people: a PI who is a board-certified sleep medicine physician, 2 postdoctoral researchers in sleep neurophysiology and computational neuroscience respectively, 3 research technicians rotating night shifts to ensure safe coverage of 8 active suites with adequate margin for event response and annotation, a clinical psychologist specializing in sleep disorders, a data engineer managing the sensor pipeline, and a study coordinator handling recruitment, consent, scheduling, and ethics compliance.

Budget is €3.8 million over 3 years, gated: €1.8M year one covering property lease and renovation at approximately €750K, equipment at approximately €450K, staff hiring accounting for German social insurance contributions and actual overnight coverage, ethics approval, and a pilot cohort of 15–20 participants; €1.2M year two covering full operations with 30–35 participants, the enhanced-night PSG program, first publications, and event-triage algorithm development; €800K year three conditional on demonstrable progress by month 24. Personnel costs are approximately €2M over three years and remaining funds cover participant compensation, independent scoring time, data storage and retention, regulatory and legal work, and contingency. Funding sources are DFG research grant and University of Bonn internal research funding as the primary pillars, with a pharmaceutical industry partnership as optional upside rather than a feasibility requirement — if secured, the academic team retains full publication rights with the pharma partner receiving a 30-day pre-publication comment window only. Success criteria at 36 months: safe residential monitoring operations sustained for at least 24 months, enrollment of 50–70 NREM parasomnia participants plus matched controls, capture of a prespecified minimum number of independently adjudicated parasomnia events sufficient for Aim 2 phenotyping analysis, 2–3 peer-reviewed publications in sleep medicine journals covering methods validation and longitudinal characterization, a benchmarked semi-automated event-triage model with reported sensitivity, specificity, and agreement against dual human scoring, de-identified physiological dataset deposited in a recognized repository, and a submitted phase-two grant proposal for expansion and intervention studies. Failure triggers: if by month 18 the facility has enrolled fewer than 25 participants or captured fewer than 40 adjudicated events, the program undergoes external review. Governance is a 3-member external scientific advisory board reviewing progress every 6 months, with the PI holding operational authority and budget reallocations above €50K requiring board approval. Banned words: AI, blockchain, app, VR, AR, gamification, wearable startup, consumer product, DAO, NFT, metaverse, smart home, autonomous diagnosis, digital therapeutic, SaaS, marketplace, validated (in the context of claiming a mature deployable system). Pick a realistic scenario — this is an early-stage clinical research facility producing methods validation and phenotyping work, not a finished diagnostic platform.

Today's date:
2026-Mar-11

Project start ASAP

**Purpose:** business

**Purpose Detailed:** Research facility establishment, data collection, and analysis for scientific publication and grant funding.

**Topic:** Establishment of a residential research unit for the study of adult NREM parasomnias

File 'plan_type.md':
This plan requires one or more physical locations. It cannot be executed digitally.

**Explanation:** This plan *unequivocally requires* a physical location (the research unit in Bonn, Germany), physical renovations, physical equipment, in-person staffing, and physical participants. The entire research project *revolves around* physical data collection and analysis in a real-world setting. The plan also requires physical travel to the location.
